risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Individual patients were randomly allocated to one of the three treatment arms using computer generated randomised permuted blocks (with randomly varying block sizes of 9, 12, 15, and 18), after stratification on the basis of whether the patient was non-ambulatory ... and whether the patient fulfilled London ME criteria." Comment: The allocation sequence was explicitly computer-generated with randomly varying permuted blocks, which is a truly random method. The description is sufficient to judge low risk for random sequence generation.

Quote: "Details of eligible patients were provided by telephone to an independent randomisation service within two working days... The random allocation was emailed to the trial manager" Comment: An independent randomisation service used by telephone, with allocation generated centrally and communicated after eligibility confirmation, indicates a central allocation mechanism that would prevent recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The trial used distinct behavioral interventions (pragmatic rehabilitation vs supportive listening vs GP usual care), with therapists clearly aware of which treatment they delivered and no indication that participants were blinded. The methods describe different session content and therapist training for each arm, making blinding of participants/personnel implausible. Because the main outcomes were self-reported fatigue, physical functioning, sleep, and mood, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence them. Comment: This creates a likely performance bias risk across the study overall.

Quote: "Assessments were performed by trained researchers blind to treatment allocation; any unblindings were recorded." Comment: Outcome assessment was carried out by blinded researchers, and the main outcomes were patient-reported scales (SF-36 physical functioning, Chalder fatigue, HADS, Jenkins sleep). Because the assessors were explicitly blinded and the outcomes were collected by those blinded researchers, the risk of detection bias from knowledge of allocation is low.

Quote: "We revised our recruitment target to 288 in September 2006 when it became clear that our loss to follow-up was less than anticipated (13% at 70 weeks)." Comment: Follow-up loss was modest overall, and the paper describes intention-to-treat analyses with inverse probability weighting to address missing outcomes. Although some participants withdrew from therapy, the authors report that missing outcome data were investigated and adjusted for, and the remaining attrition appears reasonable rather than large enough to likely bias results.
